title: Topic 224: Why Players Hate Backtracking: The Psychology of Repetition
content:
_ABSTRACT_

This paper bravely ventures into the heart of gamer discontent, a desolate landscape paved with the ghosts of slain enemies and the bitter taste of déjà vu: backtracking. We posit that the universal groan elicited by the command to “return to the starting area” is not mere petulance, but a complex psychological response rooted in cognitive load, the disruption of the sacred “flow state,” and a profound sense of existential dread. Through a rigorous, albeit hilarious, analysis of game design principles and psychological theory, we dissect the fine line between engaging repetition and soul-crushing tedium. This paper will not, unfortunately, provide a magical portal to skip the backtracking in your current game, but it will offer a deeply satisfying explanation for why you hate it so much.

_INTRODUCTION_

There is a moment in every gamer’s life that crystallizes the pure, unadulterated frustration of backtracking. For many, it was the infamous quest in *Metroid Prime: Corruption* that demanded the collection of six missile expansions to open a single door, sending players on a grand tour of previously conquered—and now painfully boring—territories. The collective sigh of a million players could have powered a small nation for a week. This paper asks the question that has echoed through the hallowed halls of gaming forums for decades: Why does backtracking, a mechanic as old as the medium itself, so often feel less like a feature and more like a punishment?

This is not a simple matter of impatience. The hatred of backtracking is a complex psychological phenomenon that lies at the intersection of repetition, reward, and cognitive load. While repetition is the very foundation of learning and mastery in games, backtracking often represents a perversion of this principle, a hollow echo of a challenge already overcome. It is the video game equivalent of being asked to un-bake a cake.

Our thesis is this: backtracking, when poorly implemented, violates the unspoken psychological contract between player and game. This contract promises a steady stream of new challenges, meaningful progression, and a respect for the player’s time. Bad backtracking breaks all of these promises, leaving the player feeling like a digital Sisyphus, forever pushing a boulder up a hill they have already conquered. This paper will explore the psychological underpinnings of this frustration, dissecting the anatomy of both good and bad backtracking with the precision of a surgeon and the wit of a stand-up comedian. We will journey through the literature on game design and psychology, analyze real-world examples from the annals of gaming history, and discuss the broader implications of our findings for understanding human behavior and consumer habits. Prepare for a deep dive into the heart of digital discontent. There will be no backtracking from here.

_LITERATURE REVIEW_

The academic world, in its infinite wisdom, has dedicated a considerable amount of time and resources to understanding why we love to play games. Psychologists, with their clipboards and knowing smiles, have identified a cocktail of powerful forces that keep us glued to our screens. The most potent of these is **Operant Conditioning**, a concept gifted to us by the venerable B.F. Skinner. Skinner, who famously taught pigeons to play ping-pong, discovered that behavior is shaped by rewards and punishments [1]. In the world of gaming, this translates to the irresistible allure of leveling up, finding a legendary sword, or simply hearing that satisfying “ding” of a completed quest. Each reward is a little hit of dopamine, a neurochemical pat on the back that says, “Good job, now do it again.”

This principle is further amplified by the power of **variable rewards**, which Skinner also observed in his feathered friends. A reward that comes at an unpredictable time is far more motivating than one that is doled out on a regular schedule [1]. This is the diabolical genius behind loot boxes and random drops; the player is kept in a perpetual state of hope, forever chasing the dragon of a rare and powerful item. It’s the same reason people pull the lever on a slot machine, and it’s a force that game developers have harnessed with terrifying efficiency.

But it’s not all about the loot. The **Self-Determination Theory**, proposed by Deci and Ryan, suggests that we are all driven by an innate need for competence and mastery [2]. Games, with their carefully calibrated difficulty curves and skill trees, provide the perfect arena for us to flex our cognitive muscles. We start as fumbling novices and gradually transform into god-like masters of our digital domains. This journey from incompetence to mastery is a deeply satisfying one, and it’s a key reason why we are willing to invest hundreds of hours in a single game.

When the challenge of a game is perfectly balanced with the player’s skill, they can enter a state of **Flow**, a concept introduced by Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi [3]. Flow is a state of complete absorption, where time seems to melt away and the player is one with the game. It’s a state of pure, unadulterated joy, and it’s the holy grail of game design. The pursuit of this elusive state is a powerful motivator for repeat play.

However, the existing literature, in its relentless optimism, has a glaring blind spot. It is so enamored with the positive aspects of repetition that it almost completely ignores the dark side of the coin: backtracking. While researchers have written volumes on the joys of mastery and the thrill of the reward, they have been conspicuously silent on the soul-crushing tedium of being forced to retrace one’s steps through a barren, monster-less landscape. This paper, therefore, is a vital corrective to the scholarly record, a lone voice crying out in the wilderness of academic complacency. We will now turn our attention to a concept that is crucial for understanding the psychology of backtracking: **Cognitive Load Theory**. This theory, which posits that our working memory is a finite resource, will be the key to unlocking the mystery of why we hate backtracking so much [4].

_ANALYSIS_

_The Psychology of "Not Again!"_

The visceral, gut-wrenching feeling of being forced to backtrack can be explained by a confluence of psychological factors, the most significant of which is **Cognitive Load Theory**. This theory, in essence, states that our brains have a limited amount of processing power at any given moment [4]. When we are learning a new skill, solving a puzzle, or fighting a new type of enemy, our brains are in a state of high-engagement, actively processing new information. This is the "sweet spot" of gaming, the state of "flow" that Csikszentmihalyi so eloquently described [3].

Backtracking, however, throws a wrench in this delicate cognitive dance. It forces the player to disengage from the process of learning and discovery and instead engage in the mundane task of navigation. The player must recall the layout of a previously explored area, a task that, while not particularly difficult, is also not particularly interesting. This is what is known as **extraneous cognitive load** – mental effort that is not contributing to the learning process. It’s the cognitive equivalent of being forced to listen to a boring story for the second time. You already know how it ends, and you’d much rather be doing something else.

This disruption of the flow state is a key reason why backtracking is so frustrating. The player is yanked out of their state of blissful absorption and forced to confront the tedious reality of their situation. The game is no longer a source of joy and discovery, but a source of frustration and boredom. The psychological contract between player and game has been broken. The player, who has invested their time and effort in the expectation of a rewarding experience, is instead met with a digital chore.

_Good vs. Bad Backtracking: A Tale of Two Corridors_

Not all backtracking is created equal. There is a fine line between a tedious slog and a clever piece of design. The Game Developer article on the subject provides a useful framework for understanding this distinction [5]. **Bad backtracking**, as exemplified by the infamous *Metroid Prime: Corruption* example, is characterized by a lack of new challenges or meaningful changes to the environment. The player is forced to traverse the same corridors, fight the same enemies (or, even worse, no enemies at all), and solve the same puzzles. It is, in short, a waste of time.

**Good backtracking**, on the other hand, is a completely different beast. It is a mechanic that is used to create a sense of discovery and empowerment. A classic example of this is the **Metroidvania** genre, which is built around the concept of backtracking. In these games, the player is constantly acquiring new abilities that allow them to access previously unreachable areas. A high ledge that was once an insurmountable obstacle can now be reached with a double jump. A sealed door can now be opened with a new weapon. This type of backtracking is not a chore, but a reward. It is a testament to the player’s growth and mastery of the game’s mechanics. The world of the game is not a static entity, but a dynamic one that is constantly changing and revealing new secrets.

Another example of good backtracking can be found in the *Dark Souls* series. The world of Lordran is a masterclass in interconnected level design. A door that was locked from one side can be opened from the other, creating a shortcut that dramatically reduces travel time. This type of backtracking is not about padding out the game’s length, but about rewarding the player for their exploration and perseverance. It is a moment of triumph, a small victory in a world that is constantly trying to crush the player’s spirit.

_REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES_

_The Good, the Bad, and the Ugly of Backtracking_

To truly understand the emotional rollercoaster that is backtracking, we must descend from the ivory tower of theory and venture into the digital trenches where these design choices play out. The history of video games is littered with examples of backtracking, ranging from the sublime to the utterly infuriating.

**The Metroidvania: Backtracking as an Art Form**

One cannot discuss backtracking without paying homage to the genre that bears its name: the Metroidvania. Games like *Super Metroid* and *Castlevania: Symphony of the Night* are the undisputed masters of good backtracking. These games present the player with a vast, interconnected world, much of which is initially inaccessible. As the player progresses, they acquire a steady stream of new abilities—the double jump, the morph ball, the grappling beam—that allow them to bypass old obstacles and unlock new areas. This is the genius of the Metroidvania: backtracking is not a chore, but a continuous cycle of discovery and empowerment. The player is not just retracing their steps; they are re-contextualizing the world, seeing it through new eyes with each new ability. The joy of a Metroidvania is not in reaching the end, but in the process of slowly and methodically peeling back the layers of the world, like a digital onion.

***Dark Souls*: The Shortcut as a Moment of Divine Intervention**

FromSoftware's *Dark Souls* series is notorious for its punishing difficulty, but it is also a masterclass in world design. The world of Lordran is a labyrinth of interconnected paths, shortcuts, and hidden passages. The player will often spend hours fighting their way through a treacherous area, only to open a door and find themselves back at a familiar bonfire. This is not a moment of frustration, but of pure, unadulterated joy. The shortcut is a reward for the player's perseverance, a hard-won victory in a world that is constantly trying to beat them down. It is a moment of respite, a brief glimmer of hope in the darkness. The backtracking in *Dark Souls* is not about padding out the game's length, but about creating a sense of place and rewarding the player for their exploration.

***The Legend of Zelda*: A Series Divided**

*The Legend of Zelda* series has a long and storied history with backtracking, and it has given us both a masterclass in how to do it right and a cautionary tale in how to do it wrong. In *A Link to the Past*, the acquisition of a new item, like the hookshot or the hammer, opens up a world of possibilities. The player is constantly revisiting old areas, but with a new sense of purpose and a new set of tools. This is backtracking at its finest. However, the series has also given us the infamous Triforce quest in *The Wind Waker*, a tedious fetch quest that required the player to sail across the Great Sea multiple times to collect a series of charts, have them deciphered, and then find the corresponding Triforce shards. This was backtracking at its worst, a blatant attempt to pad out the game's length that was so unpopular it was significantly shortened in the HD remake.

**A Cautionary Tale: *Glarth's Quest for the Mystic Muffin***

And then there are the games that get it so spectacularly wrong that they become the stuff of legend. We speak, of course, of the infamous indie title *Glarth's Quest for the Mystic Muffin*. In this game, the player, as the titular Glarth, is tasked with retrieving the Mystic Muffin from the clutches of the evil Sorcerer Steve. The game's world consists of a single, long corridor. To open the door to Steve's lair, Glarth must collect three colored keys. The red key is at the end of the corridor. The blue key is at the beginning of the corridor, but can only be reached after obtaining the red key. The green key is in the middle of the corridor, but can only be reached after obtaining the blue key. The player is forced to traverse the same, featureless corridor six times, with no new enemies, no new challenges, and no new story developments. The game is a masterclass in how to induce a state of pure, unadulterated rage. It is a monument to bad game design, a digital testament to the soul-crushing tedium of pointless repetition. Let us all learn from the tragic tale of Glarth, and vow to never again subject a player to such an ordeal.

_DISCUSSION_

_Backtracking as a Metaphor for Life, and Other Profound Musings_

The visceral hatred of backtracking in video games is not just a quirky gamer grievance; it is a window into the human psyche. The frustration of being forced to retrace one’s steps in a digital world is a reflection of our real-world aversion to wasted effort and pointless repetition. We have all experienced the “backtracking” of everyday life: the forgotten keys that force us to return home, the bureaucratic form that must be filled out for the third time, the customer service call that sends us through an endless loop of automated menus. These experiences, like their digital counterparts, are frustrating because they violate our fundamental desire for progress and efficiency.

This has profound implications for consumer behavior. In an increasingly fast-paced and competitive world, consumers have little patience for experiences that waste their time and effort. A convoluted return process, a website with a confusing layout, or a customer service system that is designed to obfuscate rather than assist are all forms of “backtracking” that can lead to customer frustration and brand abandonment. Companies that understand this and strive to create seamless, efficient, and respectful customer experiences are the ones that will thrive in the modern marketplace. The lesson from the world of game design is clear: respect the user’s time, and they will reward you with their loyalty.

_CONCLUSION_

In the grand tapestry of video game design, backtracking is a thread that is woven with both gold and straw. When used with skill and artistry, it can create a world that is rich with discovery and a sense of place. When used with laziness and contempt for the player’s time, it can transform a thrilling adventure into a tedious chore. The psychology of why we hate backtracking is a complex interplay of cognitive load, the disruption of flow, and a deep-seated aversion to wasted effort. It is a reminder that, in both the digital and the real world, the journey is just as important as the destination. And for the love of all that is holy, let that journey be a one-way trip.
references:
[1] Skinner, B. F. (1938). *The Behavior of Organisms: An Experimental Analysis*. Appleton-Century.

[2] Deci, E. L., & Ryan, R. M. (1985). *Intrinsic motivation and self-determination in human behavior*. Plenum.

[3] Csikszentmihalyi, M. (1990). *Flow: The Psychology of Optimal Experience*. Harper & Row.

[4] Sweller, J. (1988). Cognitive Load During Problem Solving: Effects on Learning. *Cognitive Science*, 12(2), 257-285.

[5] Woodbury, D. (2010, December 22). Backtracking and Non-Essential Areas. *Game Developer*. Retrieved from https://www.gamedeveloper.com/design/backtracking-and-non-essential-areas
